blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلتِّينِ وَٱلزَّيْتُونِ (١)
By the fig and the olive, (1)
وَطُورِ سِينِينَ (٢)
by Mount Sinai, (2)
وَهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ ٱلْأَمِينِ (٣)
and by this secure town: (3)
لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِىٓ أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ (٤)
We certainly created man in the best of forms; (4)
ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَـٰهُ أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ (٥)
then We relegated him to the lowest of the low, (5)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ (٦)
except those who have faith and do righteous deeds. There will be an everlasting reward for them. (6)
فَمَا يُكَذِّبُكَ بَعْدُ بِٱلدِّينِ (٧)
So what makes you deny the Retribution? (7)
أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَحْكَمِ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٨)
Is not Allah the fairest of all judges? (8)

(Commentary)

The Surah mentions the Resurrection (البعث) — the raising of the dead for judgment — and the Recompense (الجزاء) — the divine reward or punishment for one's deeds. It approaches this through the path of the creation of man in the best of forms, followed by their divergence: some remain upon their primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — while others depart from it, descending to the lowest of the low. Consequently, wisdom necessitates a distinction between the two groups in their recompense.

The Surah is Meccan, though it is possible it was revealed in Medina. The view that it was revealed in Mecca is supported by His saying: "and this secure town," though this is not definitive, as it could have been revealed after the Migration (الهجرة) while he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was in Mecca.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالتِّينِ وَالزَّيْتُونِ وَطُورِ سِينِينَ وَهذَا الْبَلَدِ الْأَمِينِ "By the fig and the olive, and by Mount Sinai, and by this secure town" (95:1-3). It has been said that the "fig" and the "olive" refer to the two well-known fruits; Allah swore by them due to their immense benefits and useful properties. It has also been said they refer to the fig and olive trees. Others have suggested that the "fig" refers to the mountain upon which Damascus is situated, and the "olive" refers to the mountain upon which Jerusalem (بيت المقدس) stands. Perhaps the names of the two fruits were applied to these mountains because they are the places where they grow, and perhaps the oath was taken by them because they were the birthplaces of a vast number of prophets. Other views have also been mentioned.

"Mount Sinai" (طور سينين) refers to the mountain upon which Allah, the Exalted, spoke to Moses bin Imran عليه‌السلام; it is also called Tur Sina.

"This secure town" (هذا البلد الأمين) refers to the honored city of Mecca, because security (الأمن) is a legislated characteristic of the Sanctuary (الحرم), and it is within it. The Exalted said: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنا حَرَماً آمِناً "Have they not seen that We have appointed a secure sanctuary" (29:67). And in the supplication of Abraham عليه‌السلام, as Allah narrated from him: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هذا بَلَداً آمِناً "My Lord, make this a secure town" (2:126), and in his second supplication: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً "My Lord! Make this city a secure one" (14:35).

The use of the demonstrative "this" (هذا) for the town serves to confirm its honor through specific identification (التشخيص). Describing it as "secure" (الأمين) is either because the word is in the form of an active participle (فاعل) meaning "possessing security," or because it is in the form of a passive participle (مفعول) meaning the town in which people are secured—that is, they do not fear calamities therein. Thus, attributing security to the town involves a type of figurative expression (تجوز).

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ "We certainly created man in the best of forms" (95:4). This is the response to the oath. The meaning of his creation being in the "best of forms" (أحسن تقويم) is that this "forming" (التقويم) encompasses him in all his affairs and aspects of existence. Taqwim (التقويم) means making a thing possess a "stature" (قوام), and the stature of a thing is that by which it subsists and remains firm. Thus, man—referring to the genus—possesses the best stature in terms of creation.

The meaning of his possessing the best stature in terms of creation—as inferred from His subsequent saying: "Then We relegated him to the lowest of the low, except those..."—is his innate capacity to ascend to the highest heights and attain an eternal, felicitous life with his Lord, free from any wretchedness. This is due to the beneficial knowledge (العلم النافع) with which Allah equipped him and the righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) He enabled him to perform. The Exalted said: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its Godwariness" (91:7-8). Thus, if he believes in what he knows and practices righteous deeds, Allah raises him to Himself, as He said: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct" (35:10). He also said: وَلكِنْ يَنالُهُ التَّقْوى مِنْكُمْ "but it is your Godwariness that reaches Him" (22:37).

He also said: يَرْفَعِ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجاتٍ "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank" (58:11), and: فَأُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الدَّرَجاتُ الْعُلى "it is such who will have the highest ranks" (20:75), among other verses indicating the elevation of man's station and his ascent through faith and righteous deeds as a gift from Allah that is never cut off. The Exalted has called this a "reward" (أجر), as indicated by His coming saying: "they shall have a reward never to be cut off."

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ رَدَدْناهُ أَسْفَلَ سافِلِينَ "then We relegated him to the lowest of the low" (95:5). The apparent meaning of "relegation" (الرد) is its well-known sense; thus, "lowest" (أسفل) is in the accusative case due to the removal of a preposition. "The lowest of the low" (أسفل سافلين) refers to a degraded station that is lower than the lowliness of the people of wretchedness and loss. The meaning is: then We returned man to a state lower than the lowliest of the people of punishment.

It is possible that "relegation" (الرد) here means "making" (الجعل)—that is, We made him the lowest of the low—or that it means "changing" (التغيير), meaning: then We changed him while he was in the state of being the lowest of all those who are low. In any case, "lowliness" (السفالة) refers to wretchedness and punishment.

It has been said that "creating man in the best of forms" refers to his physical existence during youth, characterized by the uprightness of his faculties, the perfection of his form, and the beauty of his appearance; and his "relegation to the lowest of the low" refers to his being returned to old age, whereby his outward and inward faculties are weakened and his constitution is reversed. Thus, the verse would be similar in meaning to His saying: وَمَنْ نُعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِي الْخَلْقِ "And whomever We give a long life, We cause him to regress in creation" (36:68).

However, this does not align with the exception in His saying: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ "except those who have faith and do righteous deeds" (95:6), which appears to be a connected exception (استثناء متصل). The laws of physical creation apply equally to the believer and the disbeliever, the righteous and the wicked. To claim that the believer or the righteous believer is preserved from physical aging is an unfounded assertion.

Similarly, the view that "man" refers specifically to the disbeliever, and "relegation" refers to his being cast into Hell or the reversal of his creation, with the exception being disconnected (استثناء منقطع), is also weak.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ "except those who have faith and do righteous deeds; there will be an everlasting reward for them" (95:6). That is, a reward that is never cut off. This is a connected exception from the genus of "man." The branching of the phrase "there will be an everlasting reward for them" upon this exception supports the view that "relegation to the lowest of the low" refers to wretchedness and punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: فَما يُكَذِّبُكَ بَعْدُ بِالدِّينِ أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَحْكَمِ الْحاكِمِينَ "So what makes you deny the Retribution? Is not Allah the fairest of all judges?" (95:7-8). The address is to man in terms of his genus. It has also been said it is addressed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while meaning others. The word "what" (ما) is an interrogative of reproach (توبيخية). "The Retribution" (بالدين) is connected to "deny" (يكذبك), and al-Din (الدين) here means the Recompense (الجزاء). The meaning—as has been said—is: what makes you deny the Recompense on the Day of Resurrection after We have made man into two groups: one group relegated to the lowest of the low, and another group rewarded with a reward never to be cut off?

His saying: أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَحْكَمِ الْحاكِمِينَ "Is not Allah the fairest of all judges?" (95:8). The interrogation is for affirmation (تقرير). His being the "fairest of all judges" (أحكم الحاكمين) means He is above every judge in the perfection of His judgment, its truthfulness, and its effectiveness, without any instability, weakness, or nullity. He, the Exalted, judges in His creation and His governance according to what wisdom necessitates, in terms of precision, beauty, and authority. Since Allah, the Exalted, is the fairest of all judges, and since people are of two different groups in belief and action, it is a requirement of wisdom that He distinguish between them through recompense in their eternal life, which is the Resurrection.

Thus, the consequence in His saying: "So what makes you deny the Retribution?" is like the derivation of a conclusion from an argument. His saying: "Is not Allah the fairest of all judges?" completes the aforementioned argument with that upon which its perfection depends.

In summary: if humans were created in the best of forms, then diverged such that one group departed from their best form and was relegated to the lowest of the low, while another group remained in their best form and upon their primordial nature, and Allah—the Governor of their affairs—is the fairest of all judges, then it is a requirement of wisdom that the two groups differ in their recompense. Therefore, there must be a day in which every group is recompensed for what it did, and there is no justification for denying it.

The verses—as you see—carry the same meaning as His saying: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ "Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:28), and His saying: أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَمَماتُهُمْ ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ "Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal? Evil is the judgment they make!" (45:21).

Some of those who consider the address in "So what makes you deny" to be directed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله interpret "what" (ما) as "who" (من) and "judgment" (الحكم) as "decree" (القضاء). Accordingly, the meaning would be: since people are different and this necessitates a difference in their recompense on a day prepared for it, who is it that attributes denial of the Recompense to you? Is not Allah the most decisive of all deciders? He shall judge between you and those who deny you regarding the Retribution.

As you can see, this interpretation involves unnecessary affectation (تكلف).

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالتِّينِ وَالزَّيْتُونِ — وَطُورِ سِينِينَ وَهذَا الْبَلَدِ الْأَمِينِ "By the fig and the olive, and by Mount Sinai, and by this secure town," it is stated: "The fig (التين) is Medina, the olive (الزيتون) is Jerusalem, Mount Sinai (طور سينين) is Kufa, and this secure town (هذا البلد الأمين) is Mecca."

I say: This meaning has been narrated in some traditions from Musa bin Ja'far, from his forefathers عليهم السلام, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, but it is not without its issues. In some narrations, it is said: "The fig and the olive are al-Hasan and al-Husayn, the Mount (الطور) is Ali, and the secure town (البلد الأمين) is the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله." This does not belong to the category of formal exegesis (تفسير) at all.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir bin Abdullah that Khuzaymah bin Thabit—who was not the Ansari—asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about "the secure town," and he replied: "Mecca."
